nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, the Penang Betta inhabits shallow, swiftly flowing hill streams and forest streams with sandy substrates, submerged leaf litter, exposed roots, and overhanging bank vegetation. It is also found in adjacent forest swamps and stiller backwaters. The water in these habitats is typically soft, acidic, and humic-stained — darkened by decomposing organic matter — with very little mineral hardness.

decorPreferences: dim lighting with floating plants or overhanging cover
caves or PVC tubes as retreat points
no strong current — gentle filtration only

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild, feeding on insects, their larvae, and small invertebrates. In captivity, it accepts live and frozen foods such as Daphnia, Artemia (brine shrimp), bloodworm (chironomid larvae), and mosquito larvae. Quality dried foods — micro-pellets and freeze-dried invertebrates — are generally accepted once the fish recognises them as food. The species tends toward obesity if overfed, so feeding sparingly once or twice daily is advisable.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids from similar hill-stream habitats
other small non-aggressive fish sharing blackwater parameters
avoidWith: other male Betta pugnax (intense male-male aggression)
standard community fish kept in hard alkaline water
Betta splendens and other aggressive betta species

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Betta pugnax male showing natural coloration.

summary: The Penang Betta is a wild mouthbrooding betta native to hill streams of Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, and Sumatra. Unlike the bubble-nesting Betta splendens, males orally incubate eggs for up to four weeks. Cool-tolerant (20–26°C), it needs soft acidic water and a calm, well-decorated species tank.
careNotes: The Penang Betta is available from specialist online retailers and makes a rewarding fish for aquarists comfortable with soft, acidic blackwater setups. A minimum 80 × 30 cm footprint tank (approximately 60 L) is recommended for a pair or small group of one male and two females. Water should be soft (gH 0–10, kH 0–5) and acidic (pH 4.0–7.5); adding Indian almond (catappa) leaves, alder cones, or peat extract helps replicate the tannin-rich hill-stream environment and lowers pH naturally. Temperature should be kept in the cool-to-moderate range of 20–26°C — unlike many tropical fish, the Penang Betta is tolerant of cooler temperatures reflecting its hill-stream origin and does not require sustained heat above 26°C. Gentle filtration is essential; the species tends not to tolerate strong current or turbulent flow. Lighting should be dim or diffused, and floating plants or surface cover help reduce stress and encourage natural behavior. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory, as this species is an accomplished jumper. Males will fight one another, so only one male should be kept per tank unless the aquarium is very large with clear visual barriers. Females are somewhat more tolerant of each other. Like all labyrinth fish, the Penang Betta can breathe atmospheric air directly using a specialized organ (the labyrinth organ), but good water quality remains essential regardless. It should not be kept with standard community fish that require hard, alkaline water.
breedingNotes: Betta pugnax is a paternal mouthbrooder — spawning occurs typically in a substrate depression or among leaf litter, after which the male collects and orally incubates the fertilised eggs. The male should not be disturbed or stressed during incubation as he may prematurely spit the eggs. A separate breeding tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5), leaf litter, minimal flow, and low lighting is recommended. Upon release, fry are relatively large and can accept microworms, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and vinegar eels immediately. Brood size is typically modest (20–60+ fry depending on female size). Females from Penang populations typically do not breed until above 40 mm; Singapore and Johor populations may breed at smaller sizes. Remove the male once fry are free-swimming if conspecific aggression is observed.

fish.breedingNotes: The Penang Betta is a paternal mouthbrooder: the male orally incubates the eggs for 9–16 days, with some populations carrying for up to four weeks before releasing the fry. Breeding requires a separate soft, acidic setup (pH 5.5–6.5) with leaf litter, minimal water movement, and dim lighting. Fry are released at a relatively large size and accept microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii immediately. The species is considered one of the more approachable wild mouthbrooding bettas, though it still demands a specialist approach.
